name: White Rock
short_description: BLM public rockhounding site in central Oregon's high desert featuring colorful agates, jasper, and chalcedony. No permit required, 25 lb daily limit.
description:
White Rock is a public BLM rockhounding site located in the remote high desert of Crook County, Oregon, at 5,572 feet elevation. This location sits within the John Day Fossil Beds geological zone, an area famous for Miocene-age volcanic deposits that produced diverse agate, chalcedony, and jasper specimens. The moderate slope terrain offers excellent rockhounding opportunities across exposed volcanic outcrops and wash areas where colorful stones weather out naturally.

The site attracts rockhounders seeking quality agate specimens in shades of red, orange, white, and banded patterns, along with fine chalcedony and fire-red jasper. The high desert location means exposure to sun and wind, but the elevation keeps temperatures moderate during shoulder seasons. Visitors should expect semi-arid sagebrush terrain typical of central Oregon's interior.

As BLM public land, White Rock operates under standard recreational collection rules with no permit required, making it ideal for beginners and family groups. The moderate terrain is accessible to most hikers in decent shape, though footwear with good grip is essential due to uneven volcanic rock surfaces.

Best collecting occurs in spring and fall when temperatures are comfortable and recent weather has exposed fresh material in washes and erosional areas. Summer heat can be intense at this elevation, and winter access may be compromised by snow or wet conditions.
history: This area lies within the Crooked River Valley, a region shaped by intense Miocene volcanic activity (approximately 25-17 million years ago) that deposited thick layers of silica-rich ash, tuff, and rhyolite flows. These volcanic deposits underwent silicification and weathering over millions of years, creating the agate nodules, chalcedony seams, and jasper deposits found exposed across the landscape today. The site's position near the John Day Fossil Beds reflects the rich volcanic history that defines central Oregon's geology.
tips: Search along dry washes and erosional gullies where water action naturally exposes and sorts colorful stones by size - this is where you'll find the finest agates and chalcedony. Look for banded agate specimens half-buried in red volcanic soil; the contrast makes them easier to spot. Bring a classifier or small sieve to process wash material systematically, as many pieces are small but excellent for polishing. Use a hand lens to examine specimens for color banding and translucency before collecting - quality matters more than quantity within your 25 lb daily limit.
terrain_notes: Located at 5,572 feet elevation on moderate slopes covered in sparse sagebrush and juniper. Terrain is uneven with exposed volcanic rock outcrops, loose scree, and rocky washes. Walking distances from parking vary but typically involve 0.5 to 2 miles of hiking over uneven ground requiring sturdy footwear with good ankle support. Footing is challenging due to loose volcanic rock and sharp edges; expect slow progress.
best_season: March - May and September - November. Spring offers moderate temperatures and accessible terrain after winter moisture has exposed fresh material. Fall provides similar conditions with lower humidity. Summer heat can exceed 85 degrees F at this elevation with intense sun exposure and minimal shade. Winter may bring snow at this altitude, limiting access.
written_directions: From Prineville, take US 26 east toward John Day for approximately 25 miles. Turn north onto Burnt Ranch Road (County Road 74) and continue for about 8 miles. Look for BLM marking signs and a rough pullout on the east side of the road near White Rock. The final approach may be on unimproved dirt road suitable for passenger vehicles in dry conditions. GPS coordinates are 44.424493, -120.546548.
road_conditions: Unimproved dirt road, possibly washboard in sections. Passenger vehicle suitable in dry conditions; AWD recommended after rain.
parking_notes: Rough pullout area with limited parking; no developed facility. Space for 2-3 vehicles. No amenities.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Remove only loose material; do not break rock formations or blast. Pack out all waste.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No breaking or blasting rock formations
Pack out all waste
nearest_services: Prineville, approximately 30 miles southwest. Gas, food, and water available. No services at site.
accessibility_notes: Uneven rocky terrain with loose volcanic material. Moderate hiking required over 0.5 to 2 miles. No wheelchair access. Sturdy footwear with good ankle support essential. Terrain unsuitable for young children or those with limited mobility.

faq.question: What types of agates and gemstones can you find at White Rock, Oregon?
faq.answer: White Rock is known for quality banded agate in red, orange, white, and cream colors, plus fine chalcedony, fire-red jasper, and occasional obsidian fragments. Specimens range from small thumb-sized pieces to palm-sized nodules suitable for polishing. The Miocene volcanic deposits here produce excellent quality stones compared to many other Oregon rockhounding sites.

faq.question: Is White Rock BLM land and do you need a permit to rockhound there?
faq.answer: Yes, White Rock is BLM public land in Crook County with no permit required for recreational collecting. You can collect up to 25 lbs per day per FLPMA rules. Personal use only - commercial collection is prohibited. This makes it a great no-hassle destination for beginners and families.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at White Rock near Prineville?
faq.answer: March through May and September through November offer the best conditions. Spring and fall have moderate temperatures ideal for hiking uneven terrain, and recent moisture helps expose fresh specimens in washes. Summer heat can exceed 85 degrees at 5,572 feet elevation, and winter snow may limit access.

faq.question: How do you get to White Rock and what kind of vehicle do you need?
faq.answer: From Prineville, take US 26 east about 25 miles, then north on Burnt Ranch Road (County Road 74) for 8 miles. The final approach is on rough BLM dirt road suitable for passenger vehicles in dry conditions; AWD is recommended after rain. Plan on 45 minutes to 1 hour drive time from Prineville.

faq.question: Is White Rock rockhounding site good for kids and families?
faq.answer: White Rock is family-friendly for kids ages 8 and up. No permit required, 25 lb daily limits make it manageable, and you can find quality specimens relatively quickly. The terrain is moderate with uneven volcanic rock, so bring sturdy shoes. The remote high desert location means limited shade, so pack plenty of water and sun protection.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should you bring to rockhound at White Rock?
faq.answer: Bring sturdy hiking boots with good grip, a small rock hammer or pry bar, a classifier or sieve to process wash material, hand lens for inspecting specimens, sunscreen, wide-brimmed hat, and at least 2 liters of water per person. A backpack with cushioning helps protect your 25 lb limit of collected stones during the hike out. The high desert sun and sparse shade make water essential.
